About ← backclose ×

I am a specialist in Digital Marketing and Communications, focused on the intersection of heritage storytelling and luxury brand strategy.

biancamariaboninsegni@gmail.com

Bologna · Milan · New Orleans · Geneva

I was born in Bologna, raised in the shadow of San Luca and cycling along via Saragozza, between Latin tutoring sessions and afternoons at Villa Spada. Since my school years I have always been drawn to literature, art, and history, without ever losing my interest in economics and geopolitics. Yet I struggled to reconcile these two sides of myself: the humanistic and creative with something more concrete and grounded in numbers, in economics. Almost by chance, I came across a degree programme at Università Cattolica that allowed me to do what students in the United States do when they choose a major and a minor: to enrol in an interfaculty course, specifically in Economics and Management for Arts, Culture and Entertainment, where I was able to study Law, Statistics, Marketing, and Political Economy interspersed with battles from medieval and ancient history (I will forever be fascinated by the Persian Wars) and art history (having always looked at the world through an aesthetic lens, being able to study beauty felt like an unexpected gift).

In general, I believe that interdisciplinarity and curiosity should be what drives us, the engine behind everything we do. Trying to look further strikes me as one of those wonderful things that allow us to see beyond the hill. As Dante's Ulysses reminds his companions: "...to live as brutes, but to pursue virtue and knowledge."

I remember a Milan to Geneva train journey that lasted more than five hours (they had cancelled the direct service and it became a true odyssey) during which I happened to sit next to an Italian PhD student in Physics at the University of Lausanne, who began talking to me about spin. From a simple question, "and what do you do?", I found myself with the book I was reading at the time covered entirely in spin diagrams, or rather a spin network representing a possible quantum state of space. All of this to say that even when something seems light-years away from us, it is always worth sitting down and listening.

During my Master's, I left for my exchange semester in New Orleans, in the United States. That period completely revolutionised the way I think, and I believe that American idealism, creativity, and optimism have had a significant impact on the person I am today. They also converted me to baseball, an extraordinarily intelligent sport that I fell in love with precisely during that time. I also loved the American university teaching method: small classes where memorisation was not required, and where it was possible to open debates and develop critical thinking instead.

As for my professional career, I began at Christie's in Milan, where I learned to navigate the world of luxury, the attention to detail, and to interact with HNWIs. From there I developed an analysis of auction market trends and consumer behaviour for my thesis. After my exchange, I moved to Geneva to work within the Richemont Group, where I worked on the Homo Faber Biennial, an event that brought together master craftspeople as well as the Group's maisons, and where I had the opportunity to learn the art of large-scale event management. Finally, I worked at Campari in Marketing, where I learned what it means to inhabit a brand's identity, to understand its internal dynamics, and to navigate both internal and external communications and content creation.

Creative DNA

Carlo Scarpa · David Hockney · Expressionism · Carlo Rovelli · Mark Twain · Han Kang · Dino Buzzati · Barbero · Rosalía · French hip-hop.

Thanks for reading, I hope you enjoy exploring the rest of the portfolio.

close ×
Experiences ← backclose ×
Campari Richemont Christie's New Orleans
03/2025, 09/2025 · Milan, Italy

Digital Media & Communications Specialist · Campari Group

At Campari Group, I basically served as the strategic custodian for Galleria Campari. My mission? Synchronizing the brand's historic Futurist legacy with a high-end digital aesthetic. I orchestrated the entire creative content strategy, managing the monthly editorial calendar and a complex newsletter ecosystem to flip historical archives into high-engagement social narratives.

Beyond the digital storytelling, I handled end-to-end communication for corporate events and institutional delegations, making sure the brand alignment was absolute at every single touchpoint. I even dove into specialized botanical research to define the aromatic identity of core ingredients for sensory branding, all while building out rigorous analytics systems to track engagement and optimize our weekly moves. I became completely obsessed with the fact that the recipe is a total state secret. For anyone interested in the scale of the operation, I highly recommend watching the Mega Factories: Campari documentary on YouTube.

But the absolute bonus of this experience? Getting to talk to the real heart of Campari: the bartenders. These people have the most incredible life stories and a total vocation for people, not just mixing cocktails, but narrating them. And, of course, I finally learned how to make the perfect Negroni, masterclass after masterclass.

I’m telling everyone: you have to read up on Count Camillo Negroni. The man was a total legend. He was this noble kid from Florence who basically walked away from a strict military school life in Italy and dipped to Wyoming in the late 1880s to be a literal cowboy. He spent years branding cattle and saddling horses at the Oxley Ranch before moving to New York during the “Golden Age of the Cocktail.” Picture him opening a fencing school on Madison Avenue and witnessing the theatricality of the world’s best bars. When he eventually moved back to Florence and asked Fosco Scarselli to swap the soda water in his Americano for gin, he didn’t just order a drink, he effectively created the Negroni as we know it. He was bringing that “frontier energy” back home. It’s the ultimate lesson in how a wild, adventurous heritage creates a global icon.

01/2024, 10/2024 · Geneva, Switzerland

Brand & Communications Coordinator · Richemont – Michelangelo Foundation

During my time with the Michelangelo Foundation, I focused on operational excellence by managing the logistical and administrative framework for Homo Faber 2024, an initiative that required coordinating international mediation across more than 40 countries for over 50,000 visitors.

I was responsible for curating and producing creative content for the Foundation's digital platforms, specifically defining the visual and educational journey for the Young Ambassadors program. I developed impact surveys and statistical profiles to evaluate the global cultural footprint of our initiatives, and acted as a primary liaison for educational programs representing the Foundation to a global community of master artisans and institutional partners.

▶ Van Cleef & Arpels, L'Automate Fée Ondine, Homo Faber 2024 ↗

09/2022, 12/2022 · Milan, Italy

Luxury Market & Client Communications · Christie's – Jewellery Department

In the Jewelry Department at Christie's, I supported the high-profile "Jewels & Watches: La Dolce Vita" auction, which achieved €1.77M in total sales. My contribution centred on coordinating professional photo shoots to create the premium digital assets and promotional materials necessary for such an event.

I conducted deep-dive market intelligence research into High-Net-Worth Individual behaviour and luxury trends to inform targeted communication strategies. Throughout the auction cycle, I was responsible for enforcing uncompromising premium standards to ensure total brand consistency across all client-facing touchpoints and digital catalogs.

01/2023, 05/2023 · New Orleans, Louisiana

Exchange Semester · Loyola University New Orleans

During my semester at Loyola University New Orleans, I maintained a 4.0/4.0 GPA, utilizing the experience as a strategic laboratory to bridge heritage storytelling with scalable business models.

In my Entrepreneurship class, I learned something Americans are truly masters of: the art of selling a product. Each of us had to develop a product based on a need we felt was “extremely necessary” at that moment. Mine? I spent my entire first month sick because my dormitory was a constant 18°C. My roommate even had a private AC unit, so I was literally surrounded. I tried to turn up the thermostat every chance I got, but it was four against one and I ended up sleeping in a sweater. I am convinced our Italian bodies just aren’t built for that kind of cold.

Naturally, I could only think of one thing: I hate AC. I ended up producing a jingle with the help of one of my roommates from Maryland who was a music major. We set the jingle to the tune of John Paul Young: “Hate is in the AC, everywhere I look around, Hate is in the AC, every car I get to ride... And I don’t know if I’m being chilly, Don’t know if I’ll survive...” I invented a smart air conditioning that, based on your vital and environmental parameters, would change the air in the room.

My classmates did the same, though they were even more talented. They would start their presentations with true acting skills, pretending to have lost something and searching their pockets until a friend entered the scene to present the “product of the century.” You would immediately want to buy it. Between the bright colors, the packages, and the slogans, I must say I learned a lot about their way of communicating and turning everything into an experience. Even my professor was a true performer. One day, he asked us: “What do you think about the budgeting assignment on Excel?” Someone in my class replied, “First I was afraid...” and the professor immediately cut him off, singing along to himself: “First I was afraid, I was petrified!”

By integrating Lean Startup principles, Design Thinking, and the Jobs-to-be-Done framework, I moved beyond theory into the pragmatic validation of new ventures. This analytical focus was balanced by the History of Modern Art, where I explored the power of social messaging through the work of Jacob Lawrence, and the History of Cinema, where I analyzed New Orleans as a sophisticated narrative asset. Examining location branding in productions like The Curious Case of Benjamin Button, American Horror Story: Coven, and The Originals provided a firsthand look at how cultural capital is transformed into global media value.

The result is a professional profile grounded in economic logic and strategic frameworks, yet driven by a pursuit of aesthetic and cultural excellence.

← backJournals
New Orleans Geneva
My Spring Semester in the Crescent City
New Orleans, 02/02/2023

It's evening and Sammy plays sweet songs on the guitar. It sounds like a lullaby. I'm just sorry not to hear the words, but that's okay too. Normally, I would be angry if someone played and sang at 12:37 am, but in this moment, I find solace. It feels like home. Despite a lifetime apart, different languages, diverse families, and lives separated by an ocean, it's beautiful how music unites us all, sheltered under the same roof and sky. Her singing lulls the four of us to sleep: Jeo, Morgan, Kate, and me. It's nice that she does it for herself, but almost unintentionally, she gives peace and serenity to us all.

New Orleans, 02/24/2023

I thought about my house, the walls, the streets I saw every day. I think about the porches, the stairs of my university. I left with the idea that it would be easy, but the nostalgia I feel makes me doubt myself. It sounds like a silly thing, but I miss sleeping in my own bed so much. A package with Italian food helped fill some of that void. As soon as I opened the jar of ragù, I almost had to sit down.

I don't understand how Americans socialize. At times, it's genuinely hard to try and talk to them. Which I regret, because sometimes beautiful conversations come out of it. For example, today, while having breakfast, I talked with my roommate from Brooklyn about the Romney vs. Obama presidential elections. Aside from the fact that understanding her accent is like the Rainbow Road in Mario Kart for me, it was still interesting.

There are so many new words to learn: quirky, jaded, petty, cagey. I've started thinking in English, but I still dream in Italian. "You wear your heart on your sleeve" is what K. tells me to explain that every emotion can be read on my face. I like that metaphor. I write to remember; as I read, I jot down words I don't know. I fill page after page.

Regardless, I am a better person than when I left. I feel like I know better what is right for me.

New Orleans, 06/05/2023

I perceive the pleasant scent of freshly washed hair, but also the tiredness of my eyes. The heavy rain pours outside, on one of those days when Nola decides she wants a moment for herself, stepping away from her usual brightness and happiness. It is precisely during such days that the trees of this peculiar city come alive. I struggle to find the right words to describe them, but they possess something both human and magical in their treelike existence. They remind me a little of the trees in Tolkien's Silmarillion.

It wouldn't be surprising if they suddenly started speaking and moving on their own. In their apparent silence, one can sense their eagerness to communicate with passersby. Particularly during the rain, I cannot resist stopping and listening to them. I observe them while feeling observed in return. As I wander around the Tulane campus, I witness their true essence. These days, they cease hiding and radiate with a vibrant life of their own. I witness trunks and leaves sprawling and draping over the pathway, reaffirming their role as guardians of St Charles Avenue. Could all of this be a mere figment of my imagination?

New Orleans, 11/05/2023

"I see trees of green, red roses too, I see them bloom, for me and you. And I think to myself what a wonderful world."

That is more or less what I've been listening to all evening before heading to Louis Armstrong International Airport.

New Orleans, I'm sorry I didn't understand you right away. I feel I owe you an apology. I didn't understand you because I didn't want to. It's like someone looking at contemporary art and commenting, "I could do that too." Contemporary art is poetry; it is raw thought. It isn't the observer who needs to change, but the way of looking. It was the same with New Orleans, I was just looking with the wrong eyes.

It is a complex city. After five months, I still haven't figured it out. I've only realized that it hurts to leave. I feel pulled here; it's like a giant magnet. Only those who have visited can understand its magic. I don't want to go. New Orleans taught me that I overthink things, "Just do it!" is what its guardian trees shout at me.

"Leaving is bittersweet." I used to struggle to translate Italian idioms into English, but now it's the other way around. The teachers here are kind. I will especially miss my Modern Art and Entrepreneurship professors. I will miss the people who are a bit out of their minds but good at heart, with a certain sense of familiarity. "Go ahead baby! Thank you honey!" A little piece of my heart will stay here forever, kept by the whimsical inhabitants of the Crescent City. I hope to return soon.

De Bello Helvetico

Helvetia omnis in viginti sex partes divisa est. Quarum unam incolunt Genavenses, qui lacui Lemanno et flumini Rhodano adiacent, urbs horologiorum et cygnorum.

Ab Helvetiis Germanicis, qui ad septentrionem habitant, natura et moribus differunt. Illi argentariis maxime dediti sunt: arcana servant, nomina non dicunt, numeris loquuntur. Hi Galli sunt montani, sed minus patriae amantes: cantoni suo et lacus ripae plus tribuunt quam patriae communis. Qui ad sinistram Lemanno habitant, aliter se gerunt quam qui ad dextram: diversi inter se sunt, quamquam sub eisdem montibus sub eodemque caelo vivant.

Geneva, August 1st, 2024

They sent me to Germany for work to visit the A. Lange & Söhne manufacture, tucked away in the green hills of Glashütte, not far from Dresden. The town itself feels like a setting from a Brothers Grimm tale: quiet and surrounded by forests - just without the evil stepmothers (nobody offered me any apples).

Inside the manufacture, it truly felt like the watchmaking version of Charlie and the Chocolate Factory. Seeing the engravings in person, the rubies used to avoid friction, and how they generate energy inside the mechanism - everything is built with an incredible sense of balance. I asked the people who work there what they listen to all day while working on those tiny, tiny pieces, and they replied: podcasts. They also told me that their eyesight has improved since working there. I don't know, I just found everything incredibly fascinating and it's amazing to think that they live in a little bubble, far away from the noise of the rest of the world.

White Holes, Theoretical Astrophysics close ×
On the existence of the impossible

A white hole is the theoretical time-reverse of a black hole. Where a black hole permits matter and light to enter but never escape, a white hole expels matter and light outward, nothing can enter. It is, in the most literal sense, the universe exhaling.

The mathematics that predict black holes, Einstein's field equations, permit white holes with equal elegance. They appear as the other mouth of a wormhole: matter compressed to infinite density at a black hole's singularity might tunnel through spacetime and erupt outward elsewhere. The Big Bang itself has been proposed as a white hole event.

What fascinates most is not the object but its logic: the idea that for every impossible entry there exists, somewhere in the equations, an impossible exit. That physics keeps a kind of balance sheet. That nothing, not even singularity, is a one-way door.

Loop quantum gravity suggests that the gravitational collapse of a star does not end in a singularity but undergoes a quantum bounce, re-emerging as a white hole in a distant future, or a distant region of spacetime. The black hole's interior is the white hole's past.

"The future is open. This is the most astonishing of all the facts about the universe.", Carlo Rovelli, White Holes

Animation guide

The blue vortex is the black hole pulling matter inward. The white eruption is the white hole expelling it outward. The connecting tunnel is the wormhole.

Flatland, On Dimensions close ×
E.A. Abbott · 1884

"I call our world Flatland, not because we call it so, but to make its nature clearer to you, my happy readers, who are privileged to live in Space."

Imagine a world of only two dimensions, length and width, no height. Its inhabitants are geometric shapes: triangles, squares, hexagons. They perceive each other only as line segments, inferring form through brightness and shadow. Depth is invisible to them; it is a rumour from another world.

When a sphere passes through Flatland, the inhabitants see only a circle that appears from nowhere, grows, then shrinks and vanishes. They cannot conceive of the geometry that produced it. The sphere's attempt to explain the third dimension, "upward, not northward", is incomprehensible. There is no word for it. No organ of perception to receive it.

The story is about the limits of perception, and how intelligence, constrained by the dimensions it inhabits, must perform a kind of imaginative leap to conceive of what lies beyond its own geometry. The square who is lifted into three dimensions weeps. Not from fear but from recognition: the whole time, there was more.

The tesseract, a four-dimensional hypercube, is to a cube what a cube is to a square. We cannot visualise it directly. We can only watch its three-dimensional shadow rotate, and feel the vertigo of a geometry our senses were not built to hold.

What might we be missing, living in our own Flatland of three dimensions?

Animation guide

Watch the sphere descend through the 2D plane, appearing as a growing circle, then vanishing. Then observe the tesseract rotating in projected 3D space.

This thing all things devours:
Birds, beasts, trees, flowers;
Gnaws iron, bites steel;
Grinds hard stones to meal;
Slays king, ruins town,
And beats mountain down.

What has it, precious? What has it?
